fact: December 26 is exactly one day after the winter solstice in the Northern Hemisphere, which means the days are already getting longer again - but only by about 1 minute!
In Antarctica, December 26 falls during the peak of summer when the sun never sets and penguins are busy feeding their fluffy gray chicks.
Tree sap moves super slowly in winter, so if you cut a maple tree on December 26 in cold places, barely any sap would drip out compared to spring when it gushes like a faucet.
Winter sunsets around December 26 often look extra orange and red because the sun sits so low in the sky that its light travels through way more atmosphere, filtering out the blue light.
December 26 always falls during Capricorn season in astrology, and interestingly, real goats grow their thickest winter coats right around this time of year.
Hibernating animals like bears have typically been asleep for about a month by December 26, and their heart rate slows down to only 8 beats per minute - slower than a sleeping human baby.
The moon often looks bigger and brighter in winter because cold air is denser and clearer than warm air, making December 26 nights perfect for moon-gazing in northern climates.
Snow that falls on December 26 in really cold places makes different sounds when you walk on it - fresh powder squeaks, but older snow crunches because the ice crystals have changed shape.
December 26 marks the time when evergreen trees like pine and spruce are typically pumping extra antifreeze chemicals into their needles to survive freezing temperatures.
In the Southern Hemisphere, December 26 is peak butterfly season - millions of colorful butterflies are fluttering around while kids in the north are bundled up in winter coats.
The Earth is actually closest to the sun around early January, so December 26 is when our planet is zooming toward its closest approach - even though it feels freezing cold up north.
December 26 falls during the time when wolves and coyotes typically have their thickest, fluffiest winter fur - they look like giant teddy bears but with sharp teeth.
